html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Forty-Three -- Complete

The Genesis Apocryphon

1QapGen

An Aramaic Retelling of Bereshit -- Cave 1 Qumran -- DSS Attested

Selected Columns Preserved

I Lamekh Saw the Child and Was Afraid -- Chanokh the Scribe Spoke Truth -- I Noakh Entered the Vessel -- I Avram Saw in a Dream a Cedar and a Palm Tree -- Melchizedek the King of Shalem Blessed Me

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls -- Cave 1 Qumran -- 1Q20 -- Aramaic Primary

One of the Seven Original 1947 Scrolls

CONCERN LEVEL: MINIMAL -- Overlap with Chanokh tradition confirms not contradicts

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

GENESIS APOCRYPHON -- 1QapGen -- SELECTED COLUMNS

GENESIS APOCRYPHON -- COLUMN 2 The Birth of Noakh -- Lamekh Is Troubled -- He Fears the Child Is from the Watchers -- He Goes to His Father Metushelakh -- Metushelakh Goes to Chanokh at the Ends of the Earth

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Genesis Apocryphon is one of the seven original Dead Sea Scrolls found in Cave 1 in 1947. It is an Aramaic retelling of Bereshit in the personal voices of the patriarchs — Noakh, Lamekh, and Avraham — and was written centuries before Christianity existed. No figure in this text is presented as a deity, a saviour, or a messiah in any sense. The minimal concern noted is not about false deity language but simply that the Birth of Noakh section in Column 2 covers the same narrative as Chanokh Chapter 71 already in this collection. This overlap confirms the authenticity and shared antiquity of both accounts — it does not introduce any problematic content. The Genesis Apocryphon has never been appropriated by Christianity in any significant way. It stands as pure ancient Aramaic covenant narrative history. Read this text as what it is: authentic pre-Christian Aramaic covenant literature with zero connection to false deity language, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 I Lamekh was afraid when I saw him -- for the child did not look like the children of men but was like the children of the holy angels.

2 And his form was different and not like us -- his eyes were like rays of the sun and his face was glorious.

3 And I Lamekh said to my wife Bathenosh: I adjure you by the Most High and by the great Lord that you tell me the truth -- is this child from one of the sons of heaven or from one of the Watchers?

4 And Bathenosh said to me: O my lord my brother, remember my pleasure -- I swear to you by the holy and great One that this seed is yours and that this conception was from you and not from any stranger or from any of the Watchers.

5 Then I Lamekh ran to my father Metushelakh and told him everything.

6 And he said to me: I will go to my father Chanokh and ask him what this means, for he is beloved and his portion is with the holy ones.

7 And Metushelakh went to the ends of the earth and found Chanokh there and said to him: O my father, O great one, tell me what this means.

8 And Chanokh the scribe of righteousness said: Hear me my son, for Creator has revealed everything to me and I know.

9 Go tell your son Lamekh that this child who has been born is truly his and is called by his name.

10 And raise him up for he will be a righteous man -- through him and his seed the whole earth will be preserved from the great destruction that is coming.

11 And Metushelakh returned to his son Lamekh and told him everything that Chanokh his father had revealed.

12 And Lamekh named the child Noakh saying: Through him will the earth be comforted after all the destruction.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Genesis Apocryphon (1QapGen) was found in Cave 1 at Qumran in 1947 and is designated 1Q20. It is an Aramaic retelling of Bereshit in the first-person voices of the patriarchs -- Lamekh, Noakh, and Avraham each narrate their own stories. Column 2 preserves the Noakh birth narrative in Lamekh's first-person voice, providing the fullest Aramaic account of the remarkable birth story that Chanokh Chapter 106 also preserves. The two accounts confirm each other and demonstrate that this narrative tradition was well established in the pre-common era Aramaic covenant literature.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves the Noakh birth narrative in Chanokh Chapters 106-107 -- the same story told here from Lamekh's first-person perspective. The Genesis Apocryphon does not contradict the Chanokh account but gives it greater personal intimacy: Lamekh's fear and Bathenosh's oath are narrated from within, making the birth narrative a family drama as well as a cosmic announcement. The convergence of the Genesis Apocryphon and the Orit Ge'ez Chanokh tradition on this story confirms its antiquity and authority within the broader covenant literary tradition.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Bereshit 5:28-29 preserves only the bare notice of Noakh's birth and his father Lamekh's naming declaration. The Genesis Apocryphon expands this brief Masoretic notice into a full narrative of Lamekh's fear, Bathenosh's oath, the consultation with Metushelakh, and the mission to Chanokh. The Aramaic expansion tradition of the Genesis Apocryphon represents the full narrative that the Masoretic text compressed to its minimum, preserving the covenant community's living memory of the Noakh birth tradition.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3 -- I adjure you by the Most High and by the great Lord that you tell me the truth -- is the child from one of the sons of heaven or from one of the Watchers? Lamekh's fear that his son was fathered by a Watcher is the personal covenant anxiety that mirrors Chanokh's cosmic concern in Chapters 6-16. The Watcher tradition is not merely cosmic narrative -- it is personal family history for the pre-flood patriarchs. The Genesis Apocryphon brings the cosmic covenant narrative of the Watchers down to the level of a husband's fear in the night.

GENESIS APOCRYPHON -- COLUMN 6 The Account of Noakh -- The Flood Covers the Earth -- Noakh Offers Burnt Offerings -- Creator Speaks to Noakh -- The Covenant of the Rainbow -- The Division of the Earth Among the Sons of Noakh

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Genesis Apocryphon is one of the seven original Dead Sea Scrolls found in Cave 1 in 1947. It is an Aramaic retelling of Bereshit in the personal voices of the patriarchs — Noakh, Lamekh, and Avraham — and was written centuries before Christianity existed. No figure in this text is presented as a deity, a saviour, or a messiah in any sense. The minimal concern noted is not about false deity language but simply that the Birth of Noakh section in Column 2 covers the same narrative as Chanokh Chapter 71 already in this collection. This overlap confirms the authenticity and shared antiquity of both accounts — it does not introduce any problematic content. The Genesis Apocryphon has never been appropriated by Christianity in any significant way. It stands as pure ancient Aramaic covenant narrative history. Read this text as what it is: authentic pre-Christian Aramaic covenant literature with zero connection to false deity language, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 I Noakh entered the vessel with all my sons and the wives of my sons and all the living creatures -- every clean animal and unclean animal and bird.

2 And the great flood covered the earth and all the high mountains under all the heavens were covered.

3 And all flesh that moved on the face of the earth perished -- birds and animals and every living creature that moved and all the sons of men.

4 And only I and those with me in the vessel survived.

5 And the waters prevailed upon the earth for one hundred and fifty days and then the waters decreased.

6 And in the seventh month and the seventeenth day of the month the vessel came to rest on the mountains of Ararat.

7 And I Noakh opened the covering of the vessel and saw the face of the earth -- the ground had dried.

8 And I went out from the vessel -- I and my sons and my wife and the wives of my sons -- and all the living creatures went out with us.

9 And I built an altar and offered burnt offerings on it and a pleasing aroma rose before Creator.

10 And Creator blessed me and my sons and said to us: Be fruitful and multiply and fill the earth.

11 And he established his covenant with me and with all living creatures that the waters shall never again become a flood to destroy all flesh.

12 And he set the rainbow in the clouds as a sign of the covenant between himself and all living creatures forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1Q20 Column 6 preserves the Flood narrative in Noakh's own first-person voice. The personal testimony -- I Noakh entered the vessel, I opened the covering, I built an altar -- gives the covenant narrative an immediacy that the Bereshit third-person account does not have. The Genesis Apocryphon confirms the Masoretic Flood chronology while adding personal details. The covenant rainbow of verse 12 is presented as Creator's own personal covenant oath to Noakh -- the first covenant explicitly grounded in a physical covenant sign.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves the Flood and covenant narratives of Bereshit 6-9 in full. The Yovelim tradition -- also preserved in the Orit Ge'ez -- provides an expanded version of the Flood calendar and the Noakh covenant that aligns closely with the Genesis Apocryphon. The triple confirmation -- Bereshit, Yovelim, and the Genesis Apocryphon -- of the Flood narrative and the covenant with Noakh establishes this as the most multiply attested event in the entire ancient covenant literary tradition.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Bereshit 7-9 provides the base narrative that the Genesis Apocryphon retells in first-person Aramaic. The covenant with Noakh -- Bereshit 9:11-17 -- is confirmed in the Genesis Apocryphon with the same theological emphasis: never again shall the waters become a flood to destroy all flesh, and the rainbow is the covenant sign. The Genesis Apocryphon adds no theological content absent from the Masoretic text -- it personalises and elaborates what Bereshit narrates briefly.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The Genesis Apocryphon's first-person narration of the Flood establishes Noakh as a covenant witness whose personal testimony confirms the covenant record. In the ancient Near Eastern literary tradition, first-person testimony carries greater authority than third-person narration. The Genesis Apocryphon reframes the Bereshit covenant narrative as direct covenant testimony -- Noakh, Avraham, and the other patriarchs speaking in their own voices about what they experienced and what Creator said to them personally.

GENESIS APOCRYPHON -- COLUMN 12 Avram Journeys to Egypt -- He Sees in a Dream a Cedar and a Palm Tree -- The Egyptians Come to Take Sarai -- The Beauty of Sarai Is Described -- The Princes of Egypt Praise Her Before Pharaoh

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Genesis Apocryphon is one of the seven original Dead Sea Scrolls found in Cave 1 in 1947. It is an Aramaic retelling of Bereshit in the personal voices of the patriarchs — Noakh, Lamekh, and Avraham — and was written centuries before Christianity existed. No figure in this text is presented as a deity, a saviour, or a messiah in any sense. The minimal concern noted is not about false deity language but simply that the Birth of Noakh section in Column 2 covers the same narrative as Chanokh Chapter 71 already in this collection. This overlap confirms the authenticity and shared antiquity of both accounts — it does not introduce any problematic content. The Genesis Apocryphon has never been appropriated by Christianity in any significant way. It stands as pure ancient Aramaic covenant narrative history. Read this text as what it is: authentic pre-Christian Aramaic covenant literature with zero connection to false deity language, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 And I Avram dreamed a dream in the night before I entered Egypt, and I saw in my dream a cedar tree and a palm tree.

2 And men came to cut down the cedar and to leave the palm tree alone.

3 And the palm tree cried out and said: Do not cut down the cedar, for we two are from one root.

4 And the cedar was spared for the sake of the palm tree and was not cut down.

5 And I Avram awoke from my dream and said to my wife Sarai: I have dreamed a dream and I am afraid.

6 And when the men of Egypt see you they will say: She is his wife -- and they will kill me and spare you.

7 Tell them you are my sister and I will live and will not be killed because of you.

8 And I came to Egypt and the princes of Pharaoh saw Sarai and praised her greatly before Pharaoh.

9 They said: How beautiful and fair is this woman -- more beautiful than all the women of Egypt.

10 Her face is lovely, how fine is the hair of her head, how beautiful are her eyes, how graceful her nose and the radiance of her face.

11 How beautiful her breast and how lovely all the whiteness of her skin -- her arms are goodly and her hands are perfect.

12 And Pharaoh heard the words of the princes and desired her greatly and sent and had her brought to him -- and Sarai was taken into the house of Pharaoh.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1Q20 Column 12 preserves the Avram in Egypt narrative in Avram's own first-person voice. The dream of the cedar and the palm tree is a significant addition to the Masoretic Bereshit account -- it provides Avram's theological and emotional preparation for the Egypt experience, grounding what appears in Bereshit as a morally ambiguous act in a prior divine warning received through dream. The detailed praise of Sarai's beauty by the Egyptian princes is one of the most elaborate physical descriptions in the ancient covenant literature.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves the Avram in Egypt narrative in Bereshit 12:10-20. The Genesis Apocryphon expands this brief Masoretic account significantly -- adding the cedar and palm dream, the detailed praise of Sarai, and the personal emotional experience of Avram. The Yovelim tradition also addresses the Avram in Egypt narrative in Chapter 13 with similar expansive treatment, confirming that the Avram covenant tradition generated substantial narrative elaboration in the pre-common era period.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Bereshit 12:10-20 preserves the bare bones of the Egypt narrative -- famine, journey to Egypt, fear about Sarai, the deception, Sarai taken to Pharaoh, the plagues, and the expulsion of Avram. The Genesis Apocryphon transforms this compressed Masoretic account into a full narrative with personal voice, dream vision, and detailed physical description. The cedar and palm dream -- absent from Masoretic Bereshit -- gives the narrative a theological framing that redeems Avram's deception as a response to divine warning rather than merely personal fear.

[CRITICAL NOTE] The physical description of Sarai in verse 9-11 is among the most extensive descriptions of a woman's beauty in any ancient covenant text. The Genesis Apocryphon's willingness to describe physical beauty in detail reflects the covenant tradition's affirmation of the goodness of Creator's creation -- the same affirmation that Shir HaShirim makes in its celebration of human beauty. The description of Sarai is not prurient but doxological: the beauty that captivates Pharaoh and his princes is the beauty of the covenant patriarch's wife, whose protection becomes the occasion for Creator's plague against Pharaoh.

GENESIS APOCRYPHON -- COLUMN 22 Avram Returns to the Land -- He Goes Up to Bethel -- He Sees the Land -- Creator Speaks to Avram -- Arise and Walk Through the Land -- Avram Builds an Altar at Hebron -- He Meets Melchizedek

CONCERN LEVEL — CONCERN NOTE: THIS TEXT CONTAINS NO FALSE DEITY LANGUAGE AND NO CHRISTIAN CONTENT WHATSOEVER. The Genesis Apocryphon is one of the seven original Dead Sea Scrolls found in Cave 1 in 1947. It is an Aramaic retelling of Bereshit in the personal voices of the patriarchs — Noakh, Lamekh, and Avraham — and was written centuries before Christianity existed. No figure in this text is presented as a deity, a saviour, or a messiah in any sense. The minimal concern noted is not about false deity language but simply that the Birth of Noakh section in Column 2 covers the same narrative as Chanokh Chapter 71 already in this collection. This overlap confirms the authenticity and shared antiquity of both accounts — it does not introduce any problematic content. The Genesis Apocryphon has never been appropriated by Christianity in any significant way. It stands as pure ancient Aramaic covenant narrative history. Read this text as what it is: authentic pre-Christian Aramaic covenant literature with zero connection to false deity language, Jesus, or the New Testament.

1 And I Avram went up from Egypt and I and Lot my nephew with me to the Negev and then to Bethel, to the place where I had first built an altar.

2 And I called there on the name of the Lord and blessed Creator Most High for bringing me back in peace to this land.

3 And Creator appeared to me in a vision of the night and said to me: Avram go up and look from the place where you are -- northward and southward and eastward and westward.

4 For all the land that you see I will give to you and to your seed forever.

5 And I will make your seed like the dust of the earth -- if a man can number the dust of the earth so shall your seed be numbered.

6 Arise and walk through the land in its length and in its breadth -- for I will give it to you.

7 And I Avram went and walked through the land -- its length and its breadth.

8 And I came to the Oaks of Mamre which is at Hebron and I built an altar there and offered burnt offerings to Creator Most High.

9 And I blessed Creator Most High who had guided me and returned me to this land in peace.

10 And Melchizedek the king of Shalem came out to meet me and brought bread and wine -- he was priest of Creator Most High.

11 And he blessed me and said: Blessed be Avram by Creator Most High who owns heaven and earth.

12 And blessed be Creator Most High who has delivered your enemies into your hand. And I gave him a tithe of all that I had.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1Q20 Column 22 preserves the covenant promise narrative to Avram and the Melchizedek encounter in Avram's first-person voice. The covenant promise -- all the land I will give to you and to your seed forever -- is narrated as a direct personal experience of divine speech in a night vision, giving the Abrahamic covenant its most intimate first-person expression in the Aramaic tradition. The Melchizedek encounter of verse 10-12 is the only Melchizedek narrative in a first-person covenant voice, and gives the encounter its warmest and most personal character.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves both the covenant promise to Avram in Bereshit 13 and the Melchizedek encounter in Bereshit 14 in full. The Genesis Apocryphon personalises both -- the covenant promise comes in a night vision to Avram personally, and the Melchizedek encounter is narrated from Avram's perspective as he receives the blessing and gives the tithe. The Orit Ge'ez and the Genesis Apocryphon agree entirely on the content of the Melchizedek encounter -- confirming its antiquity and authority within the covenant tradition.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Bereshit 13:14-17 and 14:18-20 provide the base texts for Column 22. The covenant promise to Avram -- look in all directions, for all this land I will give to you -- is here expanded with the personal emotional resonance of Avram's first-person experience. The Masoretic Melchizedek encounter is brief -- three verses -- but the Genesis Apocryphon confirms its essential elements while giving Avram's personal response: I gave him a tithe of all that I had.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10-12 -- Melchizedek the king of Shalem came out to meet me and brought bread and wine -- he was priest of Creator Most High. And he blessed me. The Genesis Apocryphon's Melchizedek is entirely earthly -- a historical king-priest who meets Avram after battle and performs a covenant blessing. There is no heavenly dimension to this encounter in the Genesis Apocryphon. This earthly Melchizedek of the Genesis Apocryphon stands in contrast to the heavenly Melchizedek of 11QMelchizedek -- the same name applied to two different covenant functions in the Qumran literature.

GENESIS APOCRYPHON -- COMPLETE

Volume Forty-Three -- The Ancient Covenant Record

𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THROUGH HIM WILL THE EARTH BE PRESERVED FROM THE GREAT DESTRUCTION

AND MELCHIZEDEK BLESSED ME AND I GAVE HIM A TITHE OF ALL THAT I HAD

Genesis Apocryphon Columns 2 and 22 -- Dead Sea Scrolls 1Q20
